name: golden-path.sh
description: Entry point / function library sourced (never executed) by all 5 golden commands (/loa /plan /build /review /ship) to detect workflow state, format the journey bar, and resolve golden-to-truename commands.
help_probe: no --help handling: `bash .claude/scripts/golden-path.sh --help` / `-h` / `help` / no-args ALL empirically return exit 0 with ZERO stdout/stderr output (verified live). File (.claude/scripts/golden-path.sh:1-1021) has no arg-parsing, no `case "$1"` dispatcher, no `"$@"` at all — it only defines `golden_*` functions after running init side-effects. Header comment (line 8-9) states 'Usage: source .claude/scripts/golden-path.sh' — designed to be sourced, not executed.
json_mode: partial: 2 of ~19 functions (golden_menu_options, golden_trajectory) accept a --json arg; the script itself has no top-level CLI so there is no script-level --json
stdout_stderr_notes: Init-time error goes to stderr (line 42). Zero tput/\033/ANSI color codes anywhere (grep confirms 0 hits) — safe for piping. Direct execution produces no stdout/stderr at all (confirmed empirically) — an agent probing this like any other script sees 'exit 0, no output' and cannot tell success from no-op from a silently-swallowed usage screen.
exit_code_notes: No documented exit-code dictionary for direct execution. File ends on a function definition (line 967-1021) so bash reports exit 0 regardless of args passed. The only intentional non-zero direct-exec path is the grimoire-dir guard at line 40-44: exit 1 if grimoire dir can't be resolved, exit 0 otherwise.
error_samples: [golden-path] ERROR: grimoire dir unresolved (path-lib init failed) — refusing phase detection against root-anchored paths (.claude/scripts/golden-path.sh:42)
evidence: empirical probe: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/golden-path.sh --help/-h/help/<noargs>` → exit 0, 0 lines stdout, 0 lines stderr in all 4 cases
worst_suspects: self_documentation: No --help/-h/help output whatsoever; the only usage doc is a source-code comment (line 8-9) an agent must Read, not something discoverable by probing the CLI.
agent_intuitiveness: Looks like an executable script (shebang, .sh) but silently no-ops on every invocation shape an agent would naturally try, with no signal that 'source it instead' is required.

name: golden_detect_workflow_state
description: Single-string 9-state workflow detector (bug_active/initial/prd_created/sdd_created/implementing/reviewing/auditing/complete/sprint_planned) that /loa uses to decide what to show the user; central function of the whole library.
help_probe: probe skipped: not a standalone CLI target — must be sourced then called as a shell function; no --help on the function itself (source read confirms .claude/scripts/golden-path.sh:467-521 has zero arg parsing).
json_mode: no — echoes one bare word to stdout (e.g. 'implementing'), no JSON envelope, no schema/version field
stdout_stderr_notes: Pure stdout, single line, no stderr on the success path. Suppresses sub-call errors with `>/dev/null 2>&1` (lines 469, 490) so a failure deep inside golden_check_ship_ready or golden_detect_active_bug is invisible.
exit_code_notes: Always echoes something and returns 0 (every branch ends with `return` after an `echo`); no failure exit path exists, so callers can't distinguish 'cleanly detected' from 'detection degraded by a swallowed jq/stat error upstream'.
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: All upstream errors (jq parse failures, missing state.json, stat failures) are swallowed via >/dev/null 2>&1 before reaching this function — a mis-detected state gives zero diagnostic trail.
composability: Only callable after sourcing the whole 1021-line file into the current shell; cannot be invoked as an isolated command or piped to directly.

name: --opt-out-check
description: The action agents/hooks use to gate 'is it currently OK to proceed without beads' — returns one of NO_OPT_OUT / OPT_OUT_VALID|<expiry>|<reason> / OPT_OUT_EXPIRED / OPT_OUT_INVALID, each carrying different operational meaning under overlapping exit codes.

name: --stats

When path-lib.sh's get_state_memory_dir() succeeds, MEMORY_DIR follows whatever LOA_STATE_DIR resolves to (observed live as .loa-state/memory) rather than the grimoires/loa/memory path CLAUDE.md documents and grimoire-index.sh directly reads.

Default mode writes grimoires/loa/REPO-MAP.md + .checksum + .run/repo-map.json; --json prints JSON only to stdout (no writes); --validate regenerates to a tmpfile and diffs against the committed REPO-MAP.md. description: Creates $LOA_DIR and the sqlite schema (memories/memory_embeddings/query_cache/search_history). Idempotent (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS). Every other subcommand's ensure_db_exists gate requires this to have run first.

name: qmd-sync.sh

help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 32 lines (verified live: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/qmd-sync.sh --help`).

name: sync
description: Iterates configured collections (memory.qmd.collections), indexes matching files via mtime-cache incremental tracking (or --force full), and shells out to the `qmd` binary if present (collection add + update).

name: post-merge-orchestrator.sh
description: Top-level pipeline entrypoint (invoked by CI post-merge workflow and manually) that runs classify→semver→version_bump→changelog→gt_regen→rtfm→tag→release→lore_promote→notify phases after a PR merges. stdout_stderr_notes: Phase progress (`[CLASSIFY] ...`, `[SEMVER] ...`, banner separators) goes to stdout via echo; `ERROR:`/`WARNING:` lines (missing args, gh unavailable) go to stderr. grep for tput/\033/color vars/tty checks across the whole family returned nothing — no ANSI-into-pipe risk.

Every phase failure is swallowed by `"phase_${phase}" || true` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1299), so the script exits 0 even when every single phase failed; only pre-flight arg validation (missing/invalid --pr/--type/--sha) produces the real non-zero exit, and that's always exit 1, never 2.

name: classify-merge-pr.sh
description: Workflow-level wrapper (Issue #668) that classifies a merged PR into cycle|bugfix|other. PRIMARY signal is the merge commit subject (in-tree, never empty); SECONDARY is `gh pr view` label enrichment, which fails-open to PRIMARY-only with a loud stderr WARN on gh failure. This is the script whose output feeds --type into post-merge-orchestrator.sh.
help_probe: exit 0, 2-line usage synopsis + 4-line description to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
json_mode: no: output is `key=value` lines (`pr_type=...`, `pr_number=...`), not JSON — differs from semver-bump.sh's JSON-to-stdout convention elsewhere in the same family
stdout_stderr_notes: Result lines (`pr_type=`, `pr_number=`) go to stdout; all WARN/ERROR diagnostics (gh failure, sha-resolution failure) go to stderr prefixed `[classify-merge-pr]` — clean separation, greppable prefix.
exit_code_notes: Matches its own docstring exactly: 0 = classified successfully, 2 = bad arguments (classify-merge-pr.sh:23-26) — unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh and release-notes-gen.sh, this script's exit-code contract has no dead/undocumented branches.
error_samples: ERROR: one of --merge-sha or --merge-msg is required (classify-merge-pr.sh:72)
[classify-merge-pr] WARN: gh pr view failed for PR #${PR_NUMBER}; falling through to subject-only classification (classify-merge-pr.sh:118)
evidence: classify-merge-pr.sh:79-85 (git log -1 --format=%s with no `-C "$PROJECT_ROOT"`, unlike siblings)
worst_suspects: composability — this is the only script in the family that does NOT source bootstrap.sh / anchor git via `-C "$PROJECT_ROOT"`; its `git log` call runs against the CLI's current working directory, so invoking it from a subdirectory (a plausible agent mistake, e.g. from inside `.claude/scripts/`) silently changes its answer instead of erroring

name: --merge-sha <sha>
description: Resolves the merge commit subject via `git log -1 --format=%s` when --merge-msg is not also given; this is the PRIMARY classification signal.
help_probe: documented in --help output first usage line: 'classify-merge-pr.sh --merge-sha <sha> [--pr-number <n>]'
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: On resolution failure, falls back to an empty subject with a stderr WARN rather than erroring — an empty subject then classifies as 'other' via classify_pr_type's fallthrough (classify-pr-type.sh:71), a silent-degrade path.
exit_code_notes: exit 2 only if the flag's value is missing entirely (classify-merge-pr.sh:52); a bad/non-existent SHA does NOT exit non-zero, it degrades to empty subject and exits 0.

name: classify_pr_type() rule chain (sourced from classify-pr-type.sh)
description: The actual verb this script performs: classify_pr_type() (classify-pr-type.sh:42-72, sourced at classify-merge-pr.sh:100) applies 5 precedence rules — label contains 'cycle' → cycle; title matches `\bcycle-[0-9]+\b` → cycle; title starts with Run Mode/Sprint Plan/feat(sprint/feat(cycle) → cycle; title starts with 'fix' → bugfix; else → other. THIS is the root cause of the documented 2026-07-10 known failure: a `release/*` branch merge subject (e.g. 'Merge pull request #1201 from org/release/v1.196.0') matches none of the cycle rules and doesn't start with 'fix', so it falls through to 'other' — there is no rule for release/* branches anywhere in the chain.
help_probe: N/A — not a CLI flag; this is internal logic invoked implicitly after arg parsing, so --help (which only documents the two mutually-exclusive input flags) says nothing about the classification rules or their release/* gap. Confirmed via source read only, not a runnable probe.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: The classification result is silent — no intermediate reasoning is logged to stdout or stderr about WHY a title was classified a given way, only the final `pr_type=` line. An agent debugging a misclassification has no trace of which of the 5 rules fired (or didn't).
exit_code_notes: Always returns via `echo` + implicit 0; classify_pr_type() never itself signals failure — 'other' is treated as a legitimate, first-class classification outcome, not an error.
evidence: classify-pr-type.sh:30-41 (5-rule precedence comment, no release/* rule)

classify-pr-type.sh:66 (`^fix` bugfix rule — release merges don't start with fix)
worst_suspects: intent_inference — the rule set has zero awareness of branch-name conventions (release/*, hotfix/*); it only inspects the merge commit SUBJECT text, so any branch-naming convention that doesn't also encode 'cycle'/'fix' in the subject text silently falls to 'other'

error_pedagogy — there is no warning, log line, or exit-code signal anywhere when a merge falls through all 4 positive rules to 'other'; the miss is completely silent from the CLI surface, only discoverable by manually reading pr_type= after the fact

name: semver-bump.sh
description: Conventional-commit semver calculator. Reads the current version (from git tag or CHANGELOG header) and the commits since it, then computes the next bump. This is the script whose default behavior (patch-bump when no feat:/fix: commits exist) is the proximate mechanical cause of the 2026-07-10 known failure once classify-merge-pr.sh has already mis-typed a release/* merge as 'other'.
help_probe: exit 0, usage + options block (6 lines) to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
json_mode: yes: always emits a single JSON object `{current, next, bump, commits}` to stdout (semver-bump.sh:11-12, 423-428) — this is the default and only output mode, not a flag-gated one
stdout_stderr_notes: Final JSON goes cleanly to stdout via `jq -n ... ` (line 423); ALL diagnostics (ERROR lines) go to stderr. Internally, parse_commits() also emits an intermediate commits-JSON array to file descriptor 3 (semver-bump.sh:186, 254) which is redirected to a tempfile rather than mixed into stdout/stderr — a slightly unusual but clean 3-stream design that keeps final stdout JSON-only and parseable.

name: --from-changelog
description: Forces the current-version source to the CHANGELOG.md `## [X.Y.Z]` header, then requires a matching git tag `vX.Y.Z` to exist as the diff anchor — this is the specific path named in the task's known-failure callout.

name: --downstream
description: Filters the commit list to app-zone only (via classify-commit-zone.sh) before computing the bump, so downstream/consumer repos don't count framework-internal commits toward their own release version.

exit_code_notes: exit 1 if the filter removes every single commit (semver-bump.sh:404-407) — this exit-1 cause is NOT listed in the script's own top-of-file exit-code docstring.

description: The most load-bearing exit-code contract in the family for the known-failure narrative — this is the ONE script whose documented dictionary is (nearly) accurate, which matters because post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_semver treats any non-zero exit as an undifferentiated 'semver calculation failed' (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:307-313), collapsing the 1-vs-2 distinction anyway.
help_probe: not reiterated in --help output itself (--help only shows usage/options, not the exit-code table — that's header-comment-only documentation, invisible to `--help` callers)
json_mode: no (exit codes are never surfaced in the JSON payload; a failure means NO json is emitted at all, just a stderr line)
stdout_stderr_notes: On any exit 1/2, nothing goes to stdout (the jq JSON build at the end never runs) — a caller must not assume 'stdout empty' means 'nothing happened', it specifically means the whole calculation aborted.
exit_code_notes: 0 = success (JSON emitted); 1 = no commits since anchor tag (326-327) or all commits downstream-filtered away (404-407, undocumented); 2 = bad CLI arg (282), missing version source in any mode (292, 296, 301-302, 312-313, 316-317).

name: highest_bump="patch" default (no error emitted — silent behavior)
description: parse_commits() initializes `highest_bump="patch"` (semver-bump.sh:190) before scanning commits; if none of the commits since the anchor tag match a recognized conventional-commit type (feat/fix/perf/refactor/chore/docs/test/ci/style/build), the bump silently stays at the initial 'patch' value with zero warning. Combined with post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_semver NEVER passing `--from-tag`/`--from-changelog` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:293-296 only conditionally adds `--downstream`), production always runs in 'auto' source mode. This is the second half of the documented 2026-07-10 mechanism: once classify-merge-pr.sh mis-typed a release/* merge as 'other' (see classify-merge-pr.sh::subcommand::classification-logic), semver-bump.sh's default-to-patch behavior is what actually produced the wrong tag.
help_probe: N/A — not an emitted error message at all; this is a silent default, discoverable only by reading source. --help gives no hint that 'no recognized commit types' silently resolves to patch rather than erroring or warning.
json_mode: yes — the resulting `{"bump": "patch", ...}` JSON is emitted looking exactly as confident/successful as any correctly-computed bump; there is no `confidence` or `matched_commits` field distinguishing 'we found real feat:/fix: commits' from 'nothing matched, this is just the fallback default'.
stdout_stderr_notes: No warning printed anywhere (stdout or stderr) when zero commits matched a known conventional-commit prefix — completely silent degrade-to-default.
exit_code_notes: exit 0 — a 'nothing recognized, defaulted to patch' run is indistinguishable by exit code from a 'genuinely detected a patch-level fix: commit' run.
evidence: semver-bump.sh:227-231 (unrecognized commit type falls to `commit_bump="patch"` via `${BUMP_MAP[$type]:-}` empty check)
post-merge-orchestrator.sh:293-296 (semver_args never includes --from-tag/--from-changelog; always auto mode in production)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy — this is the single biggest gap in the whole family: the exact mechanism of the documented real-world incident (release/* merge, no feat: commits → silent patch bump) produces ZERO error, warning, or degraded-confidence signal anywhere in stdout, stderr, or the JSON payload; the only way to have caught it pre-merge was reading source

regression_resistance — nothing in the JSON schema (`current/next/bump/commits`) distinguishes 'bump computed from N matched conventional commits' vs 'bump defaulted because 0 commits matched any known type' — a trivial `matched_commit_count` field would have made this mechanically detectable

name: release-notes-gen.sh
description: Generates release notes markdown for a given version via a 3-tier fallback: (1) extract the matching CHANGELOG.md section, (2) synthesize from PR title/body via `gh pr view`, (3) compile raw commit log. Called by post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_release to build the `gh release create --notes` body.
help_probe: exit 0, usage + options block (6 lines) to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
json_mode: no: output is Markdown to stdout by design (release-notes-gen.sh:11)
stdout_stderr_notes: All generated notes content goes to stdout; the only stderr traffic is the two required-arg ERROR lines and whatever `gh`/`git` commands emit that isn't already redirected to /dev/null (most are).
exit_code_notes: Docstring (release-notes-gen.sh:14-16) documents 0/1/2 (2 = 'CHANGELOG not found or version not found'), but — same pattern as post-merge-orchestrator.sh — grep confirms there is NO `exit 2` anywhere in the actual script body. The 3-tier fallback design means extract_changelog_section() failing (missing CHANGELOG or version) never surfaces as an error at all: it just falls through to Tier 2 (PR metadata) or Tier 3 (commit log, which itself has a further fallback to a bare 'Release v${version}.' line at release-notes-gen.sh:241 if even git log is empty) — the script is architected to ALWAYS succeed with exit 0, making the documented exit 2 permanently unreachable.
error_samples: ERROR: --version is required (release-notes-gen.sh:367)
ERROR: --pr is required (release-notes-gen.sh:372)
evidence: release-notes-gen.sh:240-243 (Tier 3's own final fallback to a 1-line stub — the fallback of the fallback)
worst_suspects: self_documentation — identical defect shape to post-merge-orchestrator.sh: exit code 2 is documented in the header comment but the multi-tier fallback design means it is structurally unreachable in the actual implementation

output_parseability — a caller cannot distinguish 'rich CHANGELOG-sourced notes' from 'nothing matched anywhere, here is a bare `Release vX.Y.Z.` stub' by exit code or any structured signal — both return exit 0 with Markdown on stdout; only the CONTENT differs

name: --version VERSION / --pr NUMBER
description: Both required together: --version selects the CHANGELOG section / tag range to extract from; --pr is used for PR-metadata synthesis (Tier 2) and is always printed verbatim in the '### Source' footer regardless of which tier produced the body.
help_probe: documented in --help output: '--version VERSION    Version to generate notes for (required)' / '--pr NUMBER          Source PR number (required)'
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: Missing-arg checks run sequentially (version checked before pr) so only ONE of the two ERROR lines is ever shown per invocation even if both are missing — an agent omitting both flags sees only the --version error, fixes it, reruns, and only THEN discovers --pr is also missing (release-notes-gen.sh:366-373).
exit_code_notes: exit 1 for either missing required flag (release-notes-gen.sh:368, 373) — no --pr format validation (unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh's numeric-only check on the same conceptual value).
error_samples: ERROR: --version is required (release-notes-gen.sh:367)
ERROR: --pr is required (release-notes-gen.sh:372)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics — sequential (not batched) required-arg validation means an agent missing multiple flags gets corrected one-at-a-time across multiple failed invocations instead of a single 'both --version and --pr are required' message

Agents run it after touching any SKILL.md and in CI.

name: --json
description: Switches all per-skill PASS/WARN/ERROR echoes off and emits a single jq-built result object on stdout at end of run (line 645-654). Used by agents/CI that need to programmatically enumerate which skills failed and why.

description: 0 = every checked skill passed (or --strict didn't promote anything); 1 = at least one ERROR-level finding across all checked skills; 2 = the invocation itself was malformed (unknown flag, or --skill named a nonexistent directory).

name: hook-guard.sh (whole-script surface)
description: Thin wrapper registered as the actual PreToolUse command in settings.hooks.json; it syntax-checks the target hook (e.g. block-destructive-bash.sh) via `bash -n` before executing it, so a git-conflict-markered or otherwise unparseable hook fails OPEN instead of bricking every Bash/Edit call. Usage: `.claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh .claude/hooks/safety/<hook>.sh [args...]`.

Because the script naively takes `$1` as the target path with NO flag parsing (line 43), passing '--help' makes `target="--help"`, and the subsequent `bash -n "$target"` (line 46) is executed as `bash -n --help` — bash itself interprets this as an (invalid) top-level CLI invocation and dumps its own multi-line GNU-bash usage banner to STDOUT before hook-guard's own WARN prints to stderr and the script exits 0. Confusing double-banner output for a simple --help request; captured verbatim in error_samples.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: The bash-usage-banner leak goes to STDOUT (bash's own usage text), while hook-guard's own diagnostic goes to stderr — an agent piping only stderr for 'error messages' would miss the (arguably more confusing) stdout banner entirely.
exit_code_notes: Exits 0 on EITHER parse-fail (line 48, deliberate fail-open) or successful `exec` handoff (line 51, in which case the exit code becomes whatever the wrapped hook exits — 0 or 2 for block-destructive-bash.sh). This means 'hook-guard.sh exited 2' is IMPOSSIBLE by its own logic — any exit 2 an agent observes came from the wrapped hook, never from the guard itself, which is undocumented as an invariant anywhere outward-facing (only inferable from source).

"No composition path: $source_slug -> $target_slug (no overlap)" (construct-resolve.sh:324, stdout not stderr)

worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: 'not found' (missing construct) and 'no overlap' (both constructs valid but incompatible) are two semantically distinct outcomes that collapse to the same exit code 1, forcing an agent to string-match stderr text to branch correctly instead of trusting the exit-code contract documented in the header.

name: --json
description: Switches every subcommand's stdout from human-readable text to a machine-parseable JSON envelope — the flag an agent should always pass when consuming output programmatically.
help_probe: documented in usage text only (construct-resolve.sh:124); no standalone probe applicable (it's a modifier, not runnable alone without a subcommand).
json_mode: yes: --json (this is the toggle for it)
stdout_stderr_notes: When set, all four subcommands emit a distinct JSON shape (resolved/construct/tier for resolve; composable/overlapping_paths for compose; array of slugs for list; slug/capabilities for capabilities) — shapes are NOT unified under one schema, so an agent parsing generically must branch on subcommand.
exit_code_notes: --json does not change exit codes, only output formatting (verified by reading all _output_match/_compose/_list/_capabilities branches — same return/exit values on both paths).
error_samples: JSON no-match envelope: `{"resolved": false, "query": $q, "error": "no match"}` (construct-resolve.sh:226)
worst_suspects: composability: each subcommand's --json schema is bespoke (different top-level keys, no shared envelope like {ok,data,error}), so a generic 'always parse with --json' wrapper an agent writes once still needs per-subcommand key knowledge — undermines the promise that --json alone is enough to script against this tool uniformly.

name: --index PATH
description: Overrides the construct index file location for a single invocation, taking priority over both the CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH env var and the default '.run/construct-index.yaml' path.
help_probe: documented in usage text (construct-resolve.sh:125); not independently probed since it requires a valid index argument.
json_mode: no (flag itself carries no format; combine with --json)
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — flag value only affects which file _load_index reads (construct-resolve.sh:139).
exit_code_notes: If PATH doesn't exist, downstream _load_index exits 3 with 'ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file' (construct-resolve.sh:142-144).
error_samples: "ERROR: Unknown flag: $1" (construct-resolve.sh:108) — thrown if --index is passed without a following value only in the sense that `$2` would be consumed unchecked; a missing value after `--index` at end-of-args triggers bash unbound-ish empty assignment (INDEX_PATH="") rather than an explicit error, since `set -u` still lets `${2:-}`-less `$2` produce an empty string here (construct-resolve.sh:106 does `INDEX_PATH="$2"; shift 2` with no existence check on $2)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: `--index` with no following value silently shifts past the end of args instead of erroring immediately at parse time, deferring the failure to the unrelated-looking 'Construct index not found' message downstream, which doesn't mention the flag was the cause.

name: CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH
description: Session-wide override for the index file location, used when an agent wants every construct-resolve.sh call in a session to target a non-default index without repeating --index on each call.
help_probe: documented in header comment only (construct-resolve.sh:21); not shown in the --help usage block itself (usage text omits env vars entirely — asymmetry between header docstring and runtime usage output).
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (env var, not output).
exit_code_notes: Same downstream exit 3 path as --index if the resolved file is missing (construct-resolve.sh:139-144).
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:142)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: the runtime `--help`/usage output (construct-resolve.sh:113-130) never mentions CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH or PROJECT_ROOT at all — an agent that only reads the live --help text (rather than the source header) has no way to discover these env vars exist.

description: Signals the index file itself is missing, unreadable, unparseable, or malformed (no 'constructs' key) — distinguishes infra/setup failures from ordinary 'not found' results (exit 1), letting an agent decide whether to bootstrap the index vs. try a different query.
help_probe: documented in header (construct-resolve.sh:18) and usage text (construct-resolve.sh:129).
json_mode: no (index-load errors are always plain stderr text, even when --json was requested — --json only takes effect after _load_index succeeds, since main() calls _load_index before dispatching to subcommands, construct-resolve.sh:472-481)
stdout_stderr_notes: All 4 distinct exit-3 error paths write to stderr only (construct-resolve.sh:142,151,155,163); nothing goes to stdout on this path even when --json was passed, so a --json-consuming agent gets an empty/absent JSON body on this failure class and must fall back to stderr text.
exit_code_notes: One code, four distinct causes: file missing (142), yq not installed (151), yq parse failure (155), missing 'constructs' key post-parse (163). All indistinguishable by exit code alone.
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:142)
"ERROR: yq required to read YAML index" (construct-resolve.sh:151)
"ERROR: Failed to parse index: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:156)
"ERROR: Index missing 'constructs' key" (construct-resolve.sh:163)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: even with --json requested, every exit-3 failure mode is plain unstructured stderr text rather than a JSON error envelope — an agent that only parses stdout-as-JSON on every call gets nothing to parse on this whole failure class and must maintain a separate stderr-text-matching path just for index-load errors.

name: ERROR: Unknown subcommand
description: Fires when an agent guesses a subcommand name that doesn't exist — including the natural guess 'help' (as opposed to '-h'/'--help'), which is the exact case this audit was asked to probe.
help_probe: confirmed live: `bash construct-resolve.sh help` -> stderr: "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: help" followed by the full usage block (also stderr), exit 1. Contrast with `--help`/`-h` which exit 0 and print ONLY the usage block (no error line).
json_mode: no — this error path never checks JSON_OUTPUT because it fires inside _parse_args before JSON_OUTPUT could even be considered relevant (construct-resolve.sh:95-99)
stdout_stderr_notes: Both the error line and the subsequent usage dump go to stderr (construct-resolve.sh:96-97, _usage uses cat>&2); stdout is empty on this path — verified live.
exit_code_notes: Exit 1 — same code as 'no match' from resolve/compose, so exit code alone can't tell an agent 'you mistyped the subcommand' apart from 'the subcommand ran fine but found nothing'.
error_samples: "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: $SUBCOMMAND" (construct-resolve.sh:96)
evidence: live probe: `bash construct-resolve.sh help` -> "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: help" + usage, exit 1 (stdout empty, confirmed via 2>/dev/null vs 1>/dev/null split)
worst_suspects: agent_intuitiveness: 'help' is the single most natural word an agent or human would try after '-h'/'--help' both work, yet it is the one spelling that produces an ERROR line instead of being treated as a help request — a foreseeable trap given the other two conventional spellings are supported.

name: list
description: Enumerates all known construct slugs from the index — cheapest, side-effect-free discovery call an agent makes to see what's installed before resolve/compose/capabilities.
help_probe: not independently probed (requires a valid index file to be meaningful and non-trivial to run safely under the read-only Bash allowlist); documented at construct-resolve.sh:120.
json_mode: yes: --json emits a JSON array of slugs vs. newline-separated plain slugs otherwise (construct-resolve.sh:407-411)
stdout_stderr_notes: All output to stdout via jq/echo (construct-resolve.sh:408,410); no stderr output on the success path; no failure path exists for `list` itself beyond the shared _load_index exit-3 errors.
exit_code_notes: Always returns 0 (construct-resolve.sh:412) — even an empty index (0 constructs) still exits 0 with an empty list/array, giving no signal that the index might be degenerate.
error_samples: N/A — list has no subcommand-specific error path; only the shared _load_index errors (see exit_code::3-index-missing) can fire before _list runs
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: an empty index (constructs: [] or a corrupted-but-parseable file) and a healthy index with real entries are both exit 0 with no warning distinguishing 'this worked and found nothing' from 'this worked and found things' — an agent must count array length itself to notice zero results.

name: capabilities <slug>
description: Shows a specific construct's aggregated_capabilities map — used by an agent deciding whether a construct can perform a given action before invoking it.
help_probe: not independently probed; documented at construct-resolve.sh:121.
json_mode: yes: --json (construct-resolve.sh:434-436)
stdout_stderr_notes: Result to stdout (construct-resolve.sh:435-439); not-found error to stderr (construct-resolve.sh:427).
exit_code_notes: 0 on found (construct-resolve.sh:441), 1 on construct not found (construct-resolve.sh:428) — but ALSO silently returns 0 with an empty `{}` capabilities map if the slug exists but has no `aggregated_capabilities` key at all (construct-resolve.sh:432, `// {}` default) — 'construct has zero capabilities' and 'construct exists but capability data was never populated' are indistinguishable.
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct not found: $slug" (construct-resolve.sh:427)
evidence: construct-resolve.sh:83-90 (arg check, 'ERROR: capabilities requires a <slug> argument' line 85, exit 1)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: the `// {}` jq default (construct-resolve.sh:432) silently masks missing capability data behind the same empty-map shape as 'this construct genuinely has no capabilities', so an agent can't tell whether to trust the negative result or suspect an index-population bug.

name: session-limit-capture.sh
description: Called (per CLAUDE.loa.md) whenever an agent observes a Claude session/usage-cap error in a tool or subagent result; parses the reset time out of the raw error text and snapshots live run/bridge/simstim state into .run/session-limit-state.json so a later hook can inject a one-shot resume reminder.
help_probe: no --help handling at all. Live probes: `--help`, `-h`, and literal `help` all fall through the arg-parsing loop's catch-all `*) shift ;;` (session-limit-capture.sh:34) with zero acknowledgment of the flag, leaving RAW empty; the script then hits the same generic empty-RAW check and prints "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" to stderr, exit 1 — identical output to running with literally no args at all. Confirmed live for --help/-h/help/no-args, all four: same stderr line, exit 1, empty stdout.
json_mode: no — output is a hand-built jq object written to a FILE (.run/session-limit-state.json), never printed to stdout; there is no --json flag and no stdout data mode at all
stdout_stderr_notes: Every message this script ever emits — success confirmation included — goes to stderr, never stdout: the success line "session-limit-capture: wrote $STATE_FILE (reset_at=$RESET_ISO)" is itself `>&2` (session-limit-capture.sh:130). Stdout is unconditionally empty on every path (success or failure), verified live for the --help/no-args probes (`2>/dev/null` produced zero bytes, exit still reported by the outer echo). No tput/ANSI usage found.
exit_code_notes: Blanket exit 1 for every failure class (missing --raw, unrecognized cap string, unparseable reset time, unparseable epoch, non-numeric epoch, snapshot-JSON build failure, atomic-write failure) — 7 distinct stderr messages all collapse to the same exit code 1; only exit 0 (successful capture) is distinguishable, and even that isn't visible on stdout, only inferable from the process exit status plus a stderr string match.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" (session-limit-capture.sh:39) — verified live for --help/-h/help/no-args
"session-limit-capture: input is not a recognized session-limit string; nothing captured" (session-limit-capture.sh:44)
"session-limit-capture: could not parse reset time (GNU-date-only; see session-limit-lib.sh)" (session-limit-capture.sh:49)
"session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
evidence: session-limit-capture.sh:19-20 (header: documents exit 1 conditions but never mentions --help)
session-limit-capture.sh:30-36 (arg loop; --help/-h/help all hit the catch-all `*) shift` with no error or usage)
session-limit-capture.sh:38-41 (empty-RAW check, the message every unrecognized-flag probe converges on)
live probe: `bash session-limit-capture.sh --help/-h/help` and no-args all four produced identical stderr text and exit 1, stdout empty in every case
worst_suspects: self_documentation: there is no help surface whatsoever — --help, -h, and help are all silently swallowed by the generic flag loop and produce a generic 'missing --raw' message with zero indication of what a valid raw string looks like, what session_limit_matches() actually recognizes, or that a resume-reminder hook is the consumer of this state file; an agent's only path to understanding the contract is reading the script source or its sibling lib file.

name: --raw '<error text>'
description: The only real input to this script — the full verbatim session-limit/usage-cap error text an agent observed, which the script parses for a reset time via session_limit_matches/session_limit_parse_reset (from lib/session-limit-lib.sh).
help_probe: no dedicated help; --raw is documented only in the header comment (session-limit-capture.sh:7) and inferred from the 'is required' error message at runtime. Both `--raw VALUE` (two-arg) and `--raw=VALUE` (single-token) forms are accepted (session-limit-capture.sh:32-33); an agent guessing either spelling gets consistent behavior.
json_mode: no (plain string argument, not JSON)
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — input flag, not output. Downstream validation errors for a badly-formed --raw value are all stderr-only (see error_samples).
exit_code_notes: Exit 1 whenever --raw is absent (39-40), unrecognized as a cap string (43-45), or has an unparseable reset time/epoch (48-59) — four distinct validation failures on this one flag, all exit 1, no differentiation.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: input is not a recognized session-limit string; nothing captured" (session-limit-capture.sh:44)
"session-limit-capture: could not parse reset epoch" (session-limit-capture.sh:53)
"session-limit-capture: parsed reset epoch is not numeric" (session-limit-capture.sh:57)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: 'input is not a recognized session-limit string' (line 44) never shows the agent what pattern WAS expected nor a pointer to session_limit_matches()'s actual regex — an agent whose captured error text is 99% right (e.g. slightly different phrasing than what Claude Code emits) gets no diff/near-miss feedback to self-correct with, only a flat rejection.

name: PROJECT_ROOT
description: Overrides where .run/session-limit-state.json is written/read relative to; defaults to $PWD, so an agent invoking this script from a different cwd than the repo root must set it explicitly or the state file lands in the wrong place.
help_probe: not documented anywhere in --help/usage (there is none) or in the header comment; only discoverable by reading source line 63.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (env var).
exit_code_notes: No dedicated validation of PROJECT_ROOT — if it points to a non-writable or nonexistent path, the failure surfaces later and generically as "session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (line 134), not as a PROJECT_ROOT-specific complaint.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: PROJECT_ROOT is a silent default-to-$PWD fallback with zero mention in any user-facing text (no --help exists to mention it in, and the header docstring omits it) — an agent running this script from a subdirectory or a worktree has no signal it should set PROJECT_ROOT until state ends up in the wrong .run/ directory.

name: 1 (undifferentiated)
description: The single failure exit code this script ever returns, covering seven semantically distinct failure causes an agent must otherwise distinguish by string-matching stderr.
help_probe: probe skipped for triggering the write-failure branch (would require manipulating .run/ permissions, outside the read-only/help-only allowlist); the other 6 causes (missing --raw / bad cap-string / unparseable reset / unparseable epoch / non-numeric epoch / jq build failure) were reachable via the permitted --help/-h/help/no-args probes, all converging on the same 'missing --raw' branch and confirming exit 1.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: All 7 failure messages are stderr-only text (session-limit-capture.sh:39,44,49,53,57,121,134); stdout is empty on every failure path, matching the live probe results.
exit_code_notes: No documented dictionary of distinct codes (contrast with construct-resolve.sh's 0/1/2/3 table) — the header docstring only says 'Exit 1 ... when --raw is not a recognized cap string or its reset time cannot be parsed' (session-limit-capture.sh:19-20), which itself undercounts the actual number of exit-1 branches in the code (7, not 2).
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" (session-limit-capture.sh:39)
"session-limit-capture: failed to build snapshot JSON (jq unavailable?)" (session-limit-capture.sh:121)
"session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
evidence: session-limit-capture.sh:19-20 (header only documents 2 of the 7 causes)
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: an agent scripting a retry-vs-give-up decision around this call has no exit-code signal to distinguish a transient/environmental failure (jq missing, disk write failure) from a permanent one (malformed input text) — both are exit 1, so a blind retry-on-failure loop would futilely re-run against unparseable input forever, while a give-up-on-failure loop would never retry a transient disk hiccup.

name: mount-loa.sh
description: Top-level installer entrypoint. Default (no flags) performs a full submodule-mode mount: acquires a PID lock, clones/updates the .loa submodule, syncs zones, writes config/manifest/checksums, and creates a git commit. Also curl|bash bootstraps itself (re-execs after downloading sibling scripts to a temp dir) when BASH_SOURCE[0] is unset.
help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 34 lines. Ran `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/mount-loa.sh --help` after static-proving compat-lib.sh (unconditionally sourced at mount-loa.sh:99 before arg parsing) has zero mutating top-level side effects (uname/sort/sed/readlink/find/stat probes only, all read-only) and the -h|--help case (mount-loa.sh:374-416) is hit and `exit 0`s inside the arg-parsing while loop, strictly before preflight()/main() ever run.
json_mode: partial: only verify_mount() and post_mount_constructs_install() support --json/structured output; the top-level mount flow is text-only with an embedded JSON error envelope on stderr via mount_error()
stdout_stderr_notes: Data (usage text, log()/step()/info()) goes to stdout; err()/mount_error()/warn() go to stderr. Heavy ANSI color usage (RED/GREEN/YELLOW/CYAN/BLUE/NC at mount-loa.sh:102-107) via printf '%b' with NO tty check (`[[ -t 1 ]]` or `tput` gate) anywhere in the file — colors are emitted unconditionally into any pipe/log capture, requiring agents to strip ANSI or set NO_COLOR themselves.
exit_code_notes: No single documented exit-code dictionary for the whole script; only the E0xx mount_error() codes (E010-E016) are enumerated (mount-loa.sh:147-156), each of which calls exit 1 uniformly — so all structured errors collapse to process exit 1, distinguishable only by parsing the JSON `code` field on stderr. Non-structured failures (e.g. `set -e` on an unexpected command) fall through the EXIT trap (mount-loa.sh:253-271) which also exits with whatever the underlying command's code was.
error_samples: 'ERROR: invalid ref '\''%s'\'' (must match [A-Za-z0-9._/-]+)' (mount-loa.sh:44, pipe-bootstrap ref validation)
'Loa already installed. Use --force flag to remount: curl ... | bash -s -- --force' (mount-loa.sh:479, non-interactive stdin + existing mount)
'Option $1 requires a value (got '\''${2:-}'\'')' (mount-loa.sh:298, require_operand guard for --branch/--tag/--ref/--constructs-pack)
'Submodule script not found at: $submodule_script\nPlease ensure Loa framework is complete or download mount-submodule.sh manually.' (mount-loa.sh:1656-1657)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: ANSI escapes unconditionally interleaved with data on stdout (no tty check), forcing every agent consumer to strip color codes before parsing log lines

name: help (bare word, no dash)
description: An agent naturally trying `bash mount-loa.sh help` (mirroring `git help`, `br help`, etc.) does NOT hit any help branch.
help_probe: probe skipped: source reading proves this is UNSAFE to execute. The arg-parsing case statement (mount-loa.sh:303-421) matches only `-h|--help` for help output; a bare `help` token falls to the wildcard `*) warn "Unknown option: $1"; shift ;;` (mount-loa.sh:417-420), is silently discarded, the loop then exits ($#==0), and execution falls through to `main "$@"` at mount-loa.sh:2301 — which performs a REAL mount (acquire_mount_lock, preflight, git remote add/fetch, git checkout from upstream, file writes, git commit, git tag). Running this probe would mutate the working repo, violating the hard safety rule.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (not executed)
exit_code_notes: Would exit via main()'s normal path (0 on success) rather than the help path — no error surfaced to the agent about the typo at all.
error_samples: 'Unknown option: help' (mount-loa.sh:418) — printed as a WARNING, not an error; script proceeds anyway
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: the single most natural agent guess for getting help (`<script> help`) silently degrades into full destructive execution instead of erroring or showing usage
intent_inference: an unrecognized token is treated as a no-op warning rather than inferred as a help request or rejected as invalid input

name: --force / -f
description: Forces remount without the interactive y/N prompt; required for any non-interactive (agent) invocation once Loa is already mounted, since the alternative path is a `read -p` that blocks forever with no stdin.
help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout (line 'Force remount without prompting')
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — flag only gates control flow
exit_code_notes: Without --force AND with `[[ -t 0 ]]` false (agent/non-tty context), preflight() calls `err "Loa already installed. Use --force flag..."` → exit 1 (mount-loa.sh:479). With a tty and no --force, `read -p` blocks awaiting y/N — a hazard for any agent harness that doesn't detect this and pass --force.
error_samples: 'Loa already installed. Use --force flag to remount: curl ... | bash -s -- --force' (mount-loa.sh:479)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: interactive-tty branch uses a blocking `read -p` with no timeout; an agent running under a pty without recognizing this pattern hangs indefinitely rather than failing fast

name: --migrate-to-submodule [--apply]
description: Dry-run (default) or --apply (executes) migration of a vendored install to submodule mode: classifies .claude/ files as framework/user-modified/user-owned via checksums.json, backs up, git rm --cached, submodule add, recreate symlinks, commit.
help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Migration:' section)
json_mode: no — human-readable classification report only (info/warn lines), no --json flag for this subcommand despite its report-like structure
stdout_stderr_notes: All classification report lines go through info()/warn() (stdout/stderr per the log helper conventions) but the report itself (framework/user_modified/user_owned file lists) has no machine-parseable delimiter — an agent must regex the human-readable headers to extract file lists.
exit_code_notes: Dry run always exits 0 (mount-loa.sh:1980) whether or not files are classified as risky; --apply exits 0 on success, or via err() (exit 1) on dirty tree / no .loa-version.json / feasibility failure. No distinct exit code differentiates 'dry-run complete' from 'nothing to migrate' (both exit 0).
error_samples: 'Working tree is dirty. Please commit or stash changes first:\n  git stash\nThen re-run: mount-loa.sh --migrate-to-submodule --apply' (mount-loa.sh:1828-1831)
'No .loa-version.json found. Is Loa installed?' (mount-loa.sh:1814)
'Migration cannot proceed. Fix the above issues and retry.' (mount-loa.sh:1895, after disk-space/permissions/network feasibility failures)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: the classification report (framework/user_modified/user_owned lists) is prose-formatted with no JSON/delimited mode, unlike verify_mount()'s --json support elsewhere in the same file

name: --vendored
description: Switches from the default submodule-mode install to legacy vendored mode (copies framework files directly into .claude/ instead of symlinking to a .loa/ submodule).
help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Installation Modes:' section)
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A

name: LOA_UPSTREAM / LOA_BRANCH
description: Override the upstream git remote URL and branch used for both the vendored sync (git fetch/checkout) and the submodule add. Primary lever for pointing an agent's mount at a fork or pinned branch without CLI flags.
help_probe: no --help handling for env vars: usage text never documents LOA_UPSTREAM/LOA_BRANCH at all (only --branch/--tag/--ref flags are documented in usage output) — an agent must read source to discover these override points.
json_mode: no
stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
exit_code_notes: Invalid/unreachable LOA_UPSTREAM surfaces indirectly through `git fetch "$LOA_REMOTE_NAME" "$LOA_BRANCH" --quiet` failing under `set -e`, which is caught only by the generic EXIT trap (E013 unexpected-failure JSON), not a dedicated error code — the underlying git stderr is not classified or surfaced distinctly from any other unexpected failure.
error_samples: 'ERROR (E013): Unexpected failure (exit code 1)' (mount-loa.sh:265, generic catch-all for e.g. an unreachable LOA_UPSTREAM)
evidence: .claude/scripts/mount-loa.sh:374-416 (help text omits these env vars entirely)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: two of the most consequential override points (which upstream repo/branch gets fetched and merged into the working tree) are entirely absent from --help output

name: mount_error() E010-E016 structured codes
description: The only place mount-loa.sh gives a stable, documented error taxonomy: E010 (no git), E011 (empty-repo commit failed), E012 (git user not configured), E013 (generic commit failure / unexpected), E014 (staging failed), E015 (bare repo), E016 (commit-policy detected, non-fatal warning variant).
help_probe: no --help handling: this dictionary is not surfaced via any --help/--list-errors flag; it exists only as inline `case` logic (mount-loa.sh:147-156) discoverable solely by reading source.
json_mode: yes: --json — each mount_error() call emits a `{"code":...,"name":...,"message":...,"fix":...}` (or with `"details"`) JSON object to stderr in addition to the colored human text (mount-loa.sh:172-177)
stdout_stderr_notes: Both the human-readable and the JSON envelope go to stderr (mount-loa.sh:158-177) — none of it is on stdout, so an agent piping stdout only will miss error detail entirely and see just the exit code.
exit_code_notes: Every code in the dictionary maps to exit 1 uniformly (mount_error always `exit 1` at line 180) — E010 through E016 are NOT distinguishable via $? alone; an agent must parse the stderr JSON's `code` field to know which of the 7 failure classes occurred. E016 alone is non-fatal (mount_warn_policy returns 0) despite sharing the same code namespace.
